reasoning:
The ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index OAS experienced a dramatic spike in early April 2025 (jumping from ~3.29 to a peak of 4.29 on April 9) due to a global market rout triggered by US tariff announcements. Following a "pause" on these tariffs by President Trump, the spread partially retreated, sitting at 4.12 as of April 11 (the last trading day before April 13). Historically, policy-induced spread blowouts feature significant short-term overshooting; once the initial panic subsides or is "paused" by policymakers, spreads tend to mean-revert downwards toward their pre-shock baseline (in this case, the 3.30–3.60 range).

For the shortest horizon (April 20), it is highly probable that the relief rally and market stabilization will pull the spread below the elevated 4.12 baseline, yielding a probability < 0.5. 

Moving into the medium term (May 13 and July 12), the relief from the pause may fade as structural economic damages or renewed negotiations surface, bringing greater uncertainty about whether spreads will tighten further or resume widening. 

For the long term (October 10), fundamental macroeconomic conditions (potential European recession vs. recovery) will dominate, pushing the probability closest to maximum uncertainty (0.50).

Consequently, my estimates reflect a baseline expectation of near-term mean reversion, with uncertainty strictly increasing (moving toward 0.50) as the forecast horizon extends.
